name: CHEMISTRY (Theory)
question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/2/3 (set-3)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 11 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into three Sections - A, B and C.
  3. Section A - Questions no. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section B - Questions no. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section C - Question no. 12 is case based question, carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is not allowed.

SECTION A
1.

Predict the products formed when CH3CHO reacts with the following reagents: (Any two)

  1. CH3MgBr and then H3O+
  2. Zn(Hg)/Conc. HCl
  3. Tollens' reagent
 2
2.

The resistance and conductivity of a conductivity cell containing 0·001 M KCl solution at 298 K are 1200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 1·5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 S cm-1. Calculate its cell constant and molar conductivity.

 2
3.

A reaction is first order in X and second order in Y.

  1. How is the rate affected on increasing the concentration of Y three times?
  2. How is the rate affected when the concentrations of both X and Y are doubled?
 2

SECTION B
4.(a)

Predict the products in the following reactions:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{MgBr}+{{\mathrm{CO}}_2\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{\text{ (b) }{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{\text{ (a) }\mathrm{DIBAL}-\mathrm H}{/tex}
 3

OR
(b)

Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their property as indicated:

  1. {tex}CH_3CHO,\;CH_3CH_2CHO,\;CH_3-\underset{\underset O{\vert\vert}}C-CH2-CH3,{/tex}
    {tex}CH_3-\underset{\underset O{\vert\vert}}C-CH_3\;(Reactivity\;towards\;HCN){/tex}
  2. {tex}CH_3COOH,\;Cl-CH_2-COOH,\;(CH\underset{(Acidic\;strength)}{{}_3)_2CH-COOH}{/tex}
 3
5.(a)

Write the major products in the following:




  3. {tex}CH_3-\underset H{\underset\vert C}=O\xrightarrow{NH_2OH}{/tex}
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Oxidation of propanal is easier than propanone. Why?
  2. How can you distinguish between Acetophenone and Benzophenone?
  3. Draw the structure of the following derivative: 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazone of Propanone.
 3
6.

Calculate the emf of the following cell:
Zn (s) | Zn2+ (0·01 M) || (0·001 M) Ag+ | Ag (s)
Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·76 V and
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = +0·80 V
[log 2 = 0·3010, log 3 = 0·4771, log 10 = 1]

 3
7.(a)

Write three differences between lyophobic sol and lyophilic sol.

 3

OR
(b)
  1. Write the dispersed phase and dispersion medium of milk.
  2. What is the cause of Brownian movement in colloidal particles?
  3. Why does physisorption decrease with increase in temperature?
 3
8.

A first order reduction takes 30 minutes for 75% decomposition. Calculate t1/2.
Given : [log 2 = 0·3, log 3 = 0·48, log 4 = 0·6, log 5 = 0·7]

 3
9.

Account for the following:

  1. Transition metals and their compounds show catalytic activities.
  2. Zn, Cd and Hg are non-transition elements.
  3. Zr and Hf are of almost identical atomic radii.
 3
10.(a)

Write the hybridisation and magnetic character of the following complexes:

  1. [NiCl4]2-
  2. [Co(NH3)6]3+
  3. [FeF6]3-

[Atomic number: Ni = 28, Co = 27, Fe = 26]

 3

OR
(b)
  1. Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: [Co(NH3)4(H2O)Cl]Cl2
  2. What is the difference between an Ambidentate ligand and a Bidentate ligand?
  3. Out of [Fe(NH3)6]3+ and [Fe(C2O4)3]3-, which complex is more stable and why?
 3
11.
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{2+} / \mathrm{M}}^{\ominus}{/tex} Cr Mn Fe Co Ni Cu Zn
-0.91 -1.18 -0.44 -0.28 -0.25 +0.34 -0.76

From the given {tex}\mathrm{E}^{\ominus}{/tex} values of the first row transition elements, answer the following questions:

  1. Why is {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Mn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Mn}}^{\ominus}{/tex} value highly negative as compared to other elements?
  2. What is the reason for the irregularity in the above {tex}\mathrm{E}^{\ominus}{/tex} values?
  3. Why is {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\ominus}{/tex} value exceptionally positive?
 3

SECTION C
12.

Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow:

Amines constitute an important class of organic compounds derived by replacing one or more hydrogen atoms of ammonia molecule by alkyl/aryl groups. Amines are usually formed from nitro compounds, halides, amides, etc. They exhibit hydrogen bonding which influences their physical properties. Alkyl amines are found to be stronger bases than ammonia. In aromatic amines, electron releasing and withdrawing groups, respectively increase and decrease their basic character. Reactions of amines are governed by availability of the unshared pair of electrons on nitrogen. Influence of the number of hydrogen atoms at nitrogen atom on the type of reactions and nature of products is responsible for identification and distinction between primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Reactivity of aromatic amines can be controlled by acylation process.

 5
(i)

Why does aniline not give Friedel-Crafts reaction?

 1
(ii)

Arrange the following in the increasing order of their pKb values:
C6H5NH2, NH3, C2H5NH2, (CH3)3N

 1
(iii)

How can you distinguish between CH3CH2NH2 and (CH3CH2)2NH by Hinsberg test?

 1
(iv)(a)

Write the structures of A and B in the following reactions:

  2. ​​​​​​{tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2{{\mathrm{CONH}}_2\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{Br}}_2/alc.\mathrm{KOH}}A\xrightarrow[{\mathrm{Pyridine}\;}]{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COCl}}B}{/tex}
 2

OR
(b)

How will you convert the following:

  1. Benzoic acid to aniline
  2. Aniline to p-bromoaniline
 2

answer_key_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/2/3 (set-3)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 11 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into three Sections - A, B and C.
  3. Section A - Questions no. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section B - Questions no. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section C - Question no. 12 is case based question, carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is not allowed.

SECTION : A
1.

Predict the products formed when CH3CHO reacts with the following reagents: (Any two)

  1. CH3MgBr and then H3O+
  2. Zn(Hg)/Conc. HCl
  3. Tollens' reagent
Solution:
  1. {tex}CH_3\underset{CH_3}{\underset\vert C}HOH{/tex} (Propan-2-ol)
  2. CH3 - CH3 (Ethane)
  3. CH3COO- (Acetate ion)
 2
2.

The resistance and conductivity of a conductivity cell containing 0·001 M KCl solution at 298 K are 1200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 1·5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 S cm-1. Calculate its cell constant and molar conductivity.

Solution:

k = {tex}\frac{1}{R}\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex}
{tex}1.5 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} Scm-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{1200} \Omega\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex}
{tex} \left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex}10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1200 cm-1
{tex}\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} = 0.18 cm-1
{tex} \Lambda_m {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\kappa}{C} \times 1000 \Omega^{-1} {/tex} cm2 mol-1
= {tex}\frac{1 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4}}{0 \cdot 001} \times 1000 \Omega^{-1}{/tex} cm2 mol-1
{tex} \wedge_m{/tex} = 150 {tex}\Omega^{-1} {/tex} cm2 mol-1 or 150 S cm2 mol-1

 2
3.

A reaction is first order in X and second order in Y.

  1. How is the rate affected on increasing the concentration of Y three times?
  2. How is the rate affected when the concentrations of both X and Y are doubled?
Solution:
  1. On increasing the concentration of Y three times, rate becomes 9 times.
    (d[Y])2 = 32 = 9
  2. When the concentrations of both X and Y are doubled, rate becomes 8 times.
    d[X](d[Y])2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 22 = 8
 2

SECTION : B
4.(a)

Predict the products in the following reactions:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{MgBr}+{{\mathrm{CO}}_2\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{\text{ (b) }{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{\text{ (a) }\mathrm{DIBAL}-\mathrm H}{/tex}
Solution:
  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{MgBr}+{{\mathrm{CO}}_2\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}}{/tex} CH3COOH (ethanoic acid)
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{\text{ (b) }{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{\text{ (a) }\mathrm{DIBAL}-\mathrm H}{/tex} CH3CHO (Ethanal)

  3. (Benzoic acid)
 3

OR
(b)

Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their property as indicated:

  1. {tex}CH_3CHO,\;CH_3CH_2CHO,\;CH_3-\underset{\underset O{\vert\vert}}C-CH2-CH3,{/tex}
    {tex}CH_3-\underset{\underset O{\vert\vert}}C-CH_3\;(Reactivity\;towards\;HCN){/tex}
  2. {tex}CH_3COOH,\;Cl-CH_2-COOH,\;(CH\underset{(Acidic\;strength)}{{}_3)_2CH-COOH}{/tex}
Solution:
  1. CH3COCH2CH3 < CH3COCH3 < CH3CH2CHO < CH3CHO
  2. (CH3)CH-COOH < CH3COOH < Cl-CH2-COOH
 3
5.(a)

Write the major products in the following:




  3. {tex}CH_3-\underset H{\underset\vert C}=O\xrightarrow{NH_2OH}{/tex}
Solution:

  1. (m-Nitrobenzaldehyde)

  2. (Cyclohexane)

  3. (Ethanal oxime)
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Oxidation of propanal is easier than propanone. Why?
  2. How can you distinguish between Acetophenone and Benzophenone?
  3. Draw the structure of the following derivative: 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazone of Propanone.
Solution:
  1. Because of oxidation of propanal involves cleavage of C-H bond which is weaker than C-C bond of propanone.
  2. On heating with NaOH and I2, acetophenone gives yellow precipitate of iodoform whereas benzophenone does not.

 3
6.

Calculate the emf of the following cell:
Zn (s) | Zn2+ (0·01 M) || (0·001 M) Ag+ | Ag (s)
Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·76 V and
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = +0·80 V
[log 2 = 0·3010, log 3 = 0·4771, log 10 = 1]

Solution:

The balanced cell reaction is:
Zn(s) + 2Ag+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ + 2Ag(s)
Eo for the cell is 0.80 V (given for {tex}\frac{Ag^+}{ Ag}{/tex}) and -0.76 V (given for {tex}\frac{Zn^{2+}}{Zn}{/tex}).
n the number of moles of electrons transferred, which is 2 in this case.
[A] is the concentration of Zn2+, which is 0.01 M.
[B] is the concentration of Ag+, which is 0.001 M.
E = {tex}E^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log {/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{[A]^m}{[B]^n}\right){/tex}
Now, substitute these values into the Nernst equation:
E = 0.80 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex}log {tex}\left(\frac{0.01}{0.001}\right){/tex}
Calculate the logarithmic term:
log {tex}\left(\frac{0.01}{0.001}\right){/tex} = log(10) = 1
Now, substitute this value back into the equation:
E = 0.80 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2} \cdot{/tex} 1 = 0.80 V - 0.02955 V = 0.77045 V
So, the emf of the given cell is approximately 0.770 V.

 3
7.(a)

Write three differences between lyophobic sol and lyophilic sol.

Solution:
Lyophobic sol Lyophilic sol
1. It is relatively unstable due to repulsion between the dispersion medium and dispersed phase (liquid- hating). 1. It is relatively more stable due to the attraction between the dispersion medium and dispersed phase (liquid- loving).
2. It is an irreversible sols. 2. It is a reversible sols.
3. It cannot be easily peptised. 3. It can be easily peptised.
3

OR
(b)
  1. Write the dispersed phase and dispersion medium of milk.
  2. What is the cause of Brownian movement in colloidal particles?
  3. Why does physisorption decrease with increase in temperature?
Solution:

Chapter Deleted from the Textbook.

 3
8.

A first order reduction takes 30 minutes for 75% decomposition. Calculate t1/2.
Given : [log 2 = 0·3, log 3 = 0·48, log 4 = 0·6, log 5 = 0·7]

Solution:

k = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{t} \log \frac{(R)_{\mathrm{o}}}{(R)}{/tex}
k = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{30} \log \frac{100}{25} {/tex}
k = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{30}{/tex}log4 = 0.046 min-1
{tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{0.693}{\mathbf{k}} {/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.693}{0 \cdot 046}{/tex}
{tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 15 min

 3
9.

Account for the following:

  1. Transition metals and their compounds show catalytic activities.
  2. Zn, Cd and Hg are non-transition elements.
  3. Zr and Hf are of almost identical atomic radii.
Solution:
  1. The catalytic activity of transition metals and their compounds can be explained by two basic facts:
    1. Owing to their ability to show variable oxidation states and form complexes, transition metals form unstable intermediate compounds. Thus, they provide a new path with lower activation energy, (Ea) for a reaction.
    2. Transition metals also provide a suitable surface for reactions to occur.
  2. Transition elements are characterized by partially filled (n-1)d subshells.
    Zn, Cd and Hg are not regarded as transition elements as they have completely filled (n-1)d subshell.
    The electronic configuration of zinc is [Ar]3d104s2.
    The electronic configuration of cadmium is [Kr]4d105s2.
    The electronic configuration of mercury is [Xe]5d106s2.
  3. Due to lanthanide contraction, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. It can be explained on the basis of shielding effect. The electrons present in inner shells, shield the outer electrons from nuclear charge, making them experience a low effective nuclear charge. The shielding effect exerted by the electrons decreases in the order s > p > d > f. The f subshell poorly shields the outer electrons from nuclear attraction, which results in more attractive pull of nucleus on outer electron. In case of post lanthanide elements like Hf, 4f subshell is filled and it is not very effective at shielding the outer shell electrons. Therefore, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii.
 3
10.(a)

Write the hybridisation and magnetic character of the following complexes:

  1. [NiCl4]2-
  2. [Co(NH3)6]3+
  3. [FeF6]3-

[Atomic number: Ni = 28, Co = 27, Fe = 26]

Solution:
  1. The central metal Ni undergoes sp3 hybridisation. It is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired e- in d orbitals.
  2. In [Co(NH3)6]3+ the oxidation state of cobalt is +3. Ammonia is a strong field ligand so it pair up 4 unpaired electron and free up 2 3-d orbitals. These 3-d orbitals are involved in hybridisation with one 4S and three 4P orbitals forming an inner orbital complex, so hybridisation of [Co(NH3)6]3+ is d2 sp3
    Since it has no unpaired electrons, [Co(NH3)6]3+ is diamagnetic.
  3. The hybridization of the central iron atom in [FeF6]3- is sp3 d2.
    The complex is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired electrons in the 3d orbitals of the iron atom.
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: [Co(NH3)4(H2O)Cl]Cl2
  2. What is the difference between an Ambidentate ligand and a Bidentate ligand?
  3. Out of [Fe(NH3)6]3+ and [Fe(C2O4)3]3-, which complex is more stable and why?
Solution:
  1.  The IUPAC name of this compound is Tetraammineaquachloro cobalt(III) chloride.
  2. Ambidentate ligands can bond to a central metal atom through two different donor atoms in separate ligand molecules (e.g., NO2-, which can bind through N or O). Bidentate ligands have two donor atoms within the same ligand molecule, allowing them to form two coordination bonds simultaneously with the metal center (e.g., ethylenediamine, which has two amino groups).
  3. [Fe(NH3)6]3+ is generally more stable than [Fe(C2O4)3]3-. Ammonia is a stronger ligand than oxalate, and the coordination number is the same for both complexes. Furthermore, the chelation effect of oxalate in [Fe(C2O4)3]3- is not strong enough to overcome the inherent strength of ammonia as a ligand. Therefore, [Fe(NH3)6]3+ is typically the more stable complex.
 3
11.
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{2+} / \mathrm{M}}^{\ominus}{/tex} Cr Mn Fe Co Ni Cu Zn
-0.91 -1.18 -0.44 -0.28 -0.25 +0.34 -0.76

From the given {tex}\mathrm{E}^{\ominus}{/tex} values of the first row transition elements, answer the following questions:

  1. Why is {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Mn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Mn}}^{\ominus}{/tex} value highly negative as compared to other elements?
  2. What is the reason for the irregularity in the above {tex}\mathrm{E}^{\ominus}{/tex} values?
  3. Why is {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\ominus}{/tex} value exceptionally positive?
Solution:
  1. Eo({tex}\frac{Mn^{2+}}{Mn}{/tex}) value highly negative as compared to other elements due to extra stability of half-filled 3d - orbitals of Mn2+(3d5).
  2. The Eo ({tex}\frac{M^{2+}}{M}{/tex}) values are not regular which can be explained from the irregular variation of ionisation enthalpies {tex}\left(\Delta_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{H}_1+\Delta_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{H}_2\right){/tex} and also the sublimation enthalpies which are relatively much less for manganese and vanadium.
  3. Electrode potential value is the sum of three factors, enthalpy of atomisation, ionisation enthalpy and hydration enthalpy. In case of copper the sum of enthalpy of atomisation and ionisation enthalpy is higher than hydration enthalpy.
    Therefore E0 Cu2+ I Cu value is exceptionally positive.
 3

SECTION : C
12.

Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow:

Amines constitute an important class of organic compounds derived by replacing one or more hydrogen atoms of ammonia molecule by alkyl/aryl groups. Amines are usually formed from nitro compounds, halides, amides, etc. They exhibit hydrogen bonding which influences their physical properties. Alkyl amines are found to be stronger bases than ammonia. In aromatic amines, electron releasing and withdrawing groups, respectively increase and decrease their basic character. Reactions of amines are governed by availability of the unshared pair of electrons on nitrogen. Influence of the number of hydrogen atoms at nitrogen atom on the type of reactions and nature of products is responsible for identification and distinction between primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Reactivity of aromatic amines can be controlled by acylation process.

 5
(i)

Why does aniline not give Friedel-Crafts reaction?

Solution:

Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl3 to form a salt / N of aniline acquires positive charge with AlCl3 and hence is a deactivating group.

 1
(ii)

Arrange the following in the increasing order of their pKb values:
C6H5NH2, NH3, C2H5NH2, (CH3)3N

Solution:

(CH3)3N < (CH3)3N < NH3 < C6H5NH2 / C2H5 NH2 < (CH3)3N < NH3 < C6H5NH2
(As no medium (aqueous /gaseous) or phase is given both answers to be considered).

 1
(iii)

How can you distinguish between CH3CH2NH2 and (CH3CH2)2NH by Hinsberg test?

Solution:

Add Hinsberg reagent (benzene sulphonyl chloride) to both the compounds. CH3CH2NH2 gives ppt. that is soluble in alkali while the ppt. formed by (CH3CH2)2 NH is insoluble in alkali.

CHEMISTRY (Theory) Time allowed:2 hours Maximum Marks:35 General Instructions : Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them: This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory. This question paper is divided into three Sections - A, B and C . Section A - Questions no. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions, carrying 2 marks each. Section B - Questions no. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions, carrying 3 marks each. Section C - Question no. 12 is case based question, carrying 5 marks. Use of log tables and calculators is not allowed. SECTION : A 1. Predict the products formed when CH 3 CHO reacts with the following reagents: (Any two ) CH 3 MgBr and then H 3 O + Zn(Hg)/Conc. Calculate its cell constant and molar conductivity. Solution: k = {tex}\frac{1}{R}\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} {tex}1.5 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} Scm -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{1200} \Omega\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} {tex} \left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1200 cm -1 {tex}\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} = 0.18 cm -1 {tex} \Lambda_m {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\kappa}{C} \times 1000 \Omega^{-1} {/tex} cm 2 mol -1 = {tex}\frac{1 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4}}{0 \cdot 001} \times 1000 \Omega^{-1}{/tex} cm 2 mol -1 {tex} \wedge_m{/tex} = 150 {tex}\Omega^{-1} {/tex} cm 2 mol -1 or 150 S cm 2 mol -1 2 3. A reaction is first order in X and second order in Y. How is the rate affected on increasing the concentration of Y three times? How is the rate affected when the concentrations of both X and Y are doubled? Solution: On increasing the concentration of Y three times, rate becomes 9 times. (d[Y]) 2 = 3 2 = 9 When the concentrations of both X and Y are doubled, rate becomes 8 times. d[X](d[Y]) 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 2 = 8 2 SECTION : B 4. (a) Predict the products in the following reactions: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{MgBr}+{{\mathrm{CO}}_2\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{\text{ (b) }{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{\text{ (a) }\mathrm{DIBAL}-\mathrm H}{/tex} Solution: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{MgBr}+{{\mathrm{CO}}_2\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}}{/tex} CH 3 COOH (ethanoic acid) {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{\text{ (b) }{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{\text{ (a) }\mathrm{DIBAL}-\mathrm H}{/tex} CH 3 CHO (Ethanal) (Benzoic acid) 3 OR (b) Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their property as indicated: {tex}CH_3CHO,\;CH_3CH_2CHO,\;CH_3-\underset{\underset O{\vert\vert}}C-CH2-CH3,{/tex} {tex}CH_3-\underset{\underset O{\vert\vert}}C-CH_3\;(Reactivity\;towards\;HCN){/tex} {tex}CH_3COOH,\;Cl-CH_2-COOH,\;(CH\underset{(Acidic\;strength)}{{}_3)_2CH-COOH}{/tex} Solution: CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 < CH 3 COCH 3 < CH 3 CH 2 CHO < CH 3 CHO (CH 3 )CH-COOH < CH 3 COOH < Cl-CH 2 -COOH 3 5. (a) Write the major products in the following: {tex}CH_3-\underset H{\underset\vert C}=O\xrightarrow{NH_2OH}{/tex} Solution: (m-Nitrobenzaldehyde) (Cyclohexane) (Ethanal oxime) 3 OR (b) Oxidation of propanal is easier than propanone. Why? How can you distinguish between Acetophenone and Benzophenone? Draw the structure of the following derivative: 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazone of Propanone. Solution: Because of oxidation of propanal involves cleavage of C-H bond which is weaker than C-C bond of propanone. On heating with NaOH and I 2 , acetophenone gives yellow precipitate of iodoform whereas benzophenone does not. 3 6. Calculate the emf of the following cell: Zn (s) | Zn 2+ (0·01 M) || (0·001 M) Ag+ | Ag (s) Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·76 V and {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = +0·80 V [log 2 = 0·3010, log 3 = 0·4771, log 10 = 1] Solution: The balanced cell reaction is: Zn(s) + 2Ag + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ + 2Ag(s) E o for the cell is 0.80 V (given for {tex}\frac{Ag^+}{ Ag}{/tex} ) and -0.76 V (given for {tex}\frac{Zn^{2+}}{Zn}{/tex} ). n the number of moles of electrons transferred, which is 2 in this case. [A] is the concentration of Zn 2+ , which is 0.01 M. [B] is the concentration of Ag + , which is 0.001 M. E = {tex}E^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log {/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{[A]^m}{[B]^n}\right){/tex} Now, substitute these values into the Nernst equation: E = 0.80 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\left(\frac{0.01}{0.001}\right){/tex} Calculate the logarithmic term: log {tex}\left(\frac{0.01}{0.001}\right){/tex} = log(10) = 1 Now, substitute this value back into the equation: E = 0.80 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2} \cdot{/tex} 1 = 0.80 V - 0.02955 V = 0.77045 V So, the emf of the given cell is approximately 0.770 V. 3 7. (a) Write three differences between lyophobic sol and lyophilic sol. Solution: Lyophobic sol Lyophilic sol 1. It is relatively unstable due to repulsion between the dispersion medium and dispersed phase (liquid- hating). 1. It is relatively more stable due to the attraction between the dispersion medium and dispersed phase (liquid- loving). 2. It is an irreversible sols. 2. It is a reversible sols. 3. It cannot be easily peptised. 3. It can be easily peptised. 3 OR (b) Write the dispersed phase and dispersion medium of milk . What is the cause of Brownian movement in colloidal particles? Why does physisorption decrease with increase in temperature? Solution: Chapter Deleted from the Textbook. 3 8. A first order reduction takes 30 minutes for 75% decomposition. Calculate t 1/2 . Given : [log 2 = 0·3, log 3 = 0·48, log 4 = 0·6, log 5 = 0·7] Solution: k = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{t} \log \frac{(R)_{\mathrm{o}}}{(R)}{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{30} \log \frac{100}{25} {/tex} k = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{30}{/tex} log4 = 0.046 min -1 {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.693}{\mathbf{k}} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.693}{0 \cdot 046}{/tex} {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 15 min 3 9. Account for the following: Transition metals and their compounds show catalytic activities. Zn, Cd and Hg are non-transition elements. Zr and Hf are of almost identical atomic radii. Solution: The catalytic activity of transition metals and their compounds can be explained by two basic facts: Owing to their ability to show variable oxidation states and form complexes, transition metals form unstable intermediate compounds. Thus, they provide a new path with lower activation energy, (E a ) for a reaction. Transition metals also provide a suitable surface for reactions to occur. Transition elements are characterized by partially filled (n-1)d subshells. Zn, Cd and Hg are not regarded as transition elements as they have completely filled (n-1)d subshell. The electronic configuration of zinc is [Ar]3d 10 4s 2 . The electronic configuration of cadmium is [Kr]4d 10 5s 2 . The electronic configuration of mercury is [Xe]5d 10 6s 2 . Due to lanthanide contraction, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. It can be explained on the basis of shielding effect. The electrons present in inner shells, shield the outer electrons from nuclear charge, making them experience a low effective nuclear charge. The shielding effect exerted by the electrons decreases in the order s > p > d > f. The f subshell poorly shields the outer electrons from nuclear attraction, which results in more attractive pull of nucleus on outer electron. In case of post lanthanide elements like Hf, 4f subshell is filled and it is not very effective at shielding the outer shell electrons. Therefore, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. 3 10. (a) Write the hybridisation and magnetic character of the following complexes: [NiCl 4 ] 2- [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ [FeF 6 ] 3- [Atomic number: Ni = 28, Co = 27, Fe = 26] Solution: The central metal Ni undergoes sp 3 hybridisation. It is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired e - in d orbitals. In [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ the oxidation state of cobalt is +3. Ammonia is a strong field ligand so it pair up 4 unpaired electron and free up 2 3-d orbitals. These 3-d orbitals are involved in hybridisation with one 4S and three 4P orbitals forming an inner orbital complex, so hybridisation of [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is d 2 sp 3 Since it has no unpaired electrons, [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is diamagnetic. The hybridization of the central iron atom in [FeF 6 ] 3 - is sp 3 d 2 . The complex is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired electrons in the 3d orbitals of the iron atom. 3 OR (b) Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: [Co(NH 3 ) 4 (H 2 O)Cl]Cl 2 What is the difference between an Ambidentate ligand and a Bidentate ligand? Out of [Fe(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ and [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- , which complex is more stable and why? Solution: The IUPAC name of this compound is Tetraammineaquachloro cobalt(III) chloride. Ambidentate ligands can bond to a central metal atom through two different donor atoms in separate ligand molecules (e.g., NO 2- , which can bind through N or O). Bidentate ligands have two donor atoms within the same ligand molecule, allowing them to form two coordination bonds simultaneously with the metal center (e.g., ethylenediamine, which has two amino groups). [Fe(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is generally more stable than [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- . Ammonia is a stronger ligand than oxalate, and the coordination number is the same for both complexes. Furthermore, the chelation effect of oxalate in [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- is not strong enough to overcome the inherent strength of ammonia as a ligand. Therefore, [Fe(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is typically the more stable complex. 3 11. {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{2+} / \mathrm{M}}^{\ominus}{/tex} Cr Mn Fe Co Ni Cu Zn -0.91 -1.18 -0.44 -0.28 -0.25 +0.34 -0.76 From the given {tex}\mathrm{E}^{\ominus}{/tex} values of the first row transition elements, answer the following questions: Why is {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Mn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Mn}}^{\ominus}{/tex} value highly negative as compared to other elements? What is the reason for the irregularity in the above {tex}\mathrm{E}^{\ominus}{/tex} values? Why is {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\ominus}{/tex} value exceptionally positive? Solution: E o ( {tex}\frac{Mn^{2+}}{Mn}{/tex} ) value highly negative as compared to other elements due to extra stability of half-filled 3d - orbitals of Mn 2+ (3d 5 ). The E o ( {tex}\frac{M^{2+}}{M}{/tex} ) values are not regular which can be explained from the irregular variation of ionisation enthalpies {tex}\left(\Delta_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{H}_1+\Delta_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{H}_2\right){/tex} and also the sublimation enthalpies which are relatively much less for manganese and vanadium. Electrode potential value is the sum of three factors, enthalpy of atomisation, ionisation enthalpy and hydration enthalpy. In case of copper the sum of enthalpy of atomisation and ionisation enthalpy is higher than hydration enthalpy. Therefore E 0 Cu 2+ I Cu value is exceptionally positive. 3 SECTION : C 12.
